skill.name: image-inpainting
skill.description: Use RunComfy image inpainting models for mask-driven local edits, object removal, background replacement, and described region edits through the runcomfy CLI.
skill.summary: Run mask-based and description-based image inpainting jobs with RunComfy models.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most were false positives from Markdown code spans, model names, and prose. The confirmed risks are scoped external CLI execution, network processing through RunComfy endpoints, and local API token storage, so the skill needs a user-visible privacy and credential warning but is not blocked.

medium_findings.title: Networked Third-Party Image Processing
medium_findings.description: The skill sends image, mask, and prompt data to RunComfy model endpoints through the runcomfy CLI. This is expected behavior, but users must understand that source media and edit instructions leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that the CLI posts to the Model API and downloads results. This confirms network processing, although the endpoints are documented and allowlisted.

medium_findings.title: Scoped External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash commands matching runcomfy and provides multiple CLI invocation examples. The scope is limited to RunComfy commands, but the skill still depends on an external executable that can perform network operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration restricts Bash usage to runcomfy commands. The risk is real because commands execute locally, but no arbitrary shell pattern or user-controlled command construction was found.

low_findings.title: Local API Token Storage
low_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes an API token under the user config directory with mode 0600. This is normal credential storage, but users should protect local profiles and prefer environment variables in CI.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and file mode are explicitly documented. The 0600 permission note reduces severity, but credential material is still stored on disk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are inline Markdown code spans, model identifiers, table fields, or fenced examples. I found no Ruby code, no dynamic command construction, and no hidden execution path outside the documented runcomfy CLI.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation, and the flagged backticks are formatting markers or command examples. There is no Ruby source file or backtick execution context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Flags Are Prose False Positives
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography alerts map to descriptive text, model names, and workflow guidance. The reconnaissance alerts map to repeated guidance using the word avoid, not system probing commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic API, hashing operation, network scanning command, or host reconnaissance command appears in the reviewed lines. The matches are semantic noise from Markdown text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Service References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to RunComfy documentation, model catalog pages, related skills, and placeholder user CDN examples. They do not show covert exfiltration or unknown third-party destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links or explicit image input examples. The only confirmed network behavior is the intended RunComfy model workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Access Limited to runcomfy
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for runcomfy commands only. This is a constrained but meaningful execution capability that depends on the installed CLI and user authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line is explicit and narrow. It confirms external command access but also provides a strong scope limit.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Model API Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI posts job data to RunComfy model infrastructure, polls status, and downloads generated results. This is necessary for the skill but should be disclosed to users handling sensitive images.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is described directly in the security and workflow sections. The documented allowlist reduces uncertainty about destination scope.

content.value_statement: Precise image repairs can be hard when only one region should change. This skill routes masked and described edits to RunComfy inpainting models through a clear CLI workflow.
actual_capabilities: Routes mask-based jobs to Z-Image Turbo Inpainting.
Uses description-based edit models when no mask is available.
Provides CLI examples for object removal and background replacement.
Explains input fields such as image, mask image, prompt, strength, and control scale.
Documents model selection guidance for several RunComfy edit routes.
Lists security and privacy boundaries for CLI use.
limitations: Requires the runcomfy CLI and a RunComfy login or token.
Requires hosted image and mask URLs for mask-based inpainting.
Does not create masks inside the skill.
Does not perform video inpainting or canvas outpainting.

use_cases.title: Clean Product Photos
use_cases.description: Replace a masked background or remove unwanted objects while preserving the product area.

use_cases.title: Repair Marketing Images
use_cases.description: Fill small damaged regions, remove distractions, or retouch blemishes with controlled strength settings.

use_cases.title: Prototype Local Edits
use_cases.description: Compare mask-based and description-based model routes before building an automated image workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Remove a Simple Object
prompt_templates.prompt: Inpaint this image using the provided mask. Remove the selected object and preserve the surrounding texture, lighting, and perspective.

prompt_templates.title: Replace a Background
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mask to replace only the background with a clean studio backdrop. Keep the product shape, shadows, and color unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Edit Without a Mask
prompt_templates.prompt: No mask is available. Remove the item described in the lower-right area and keep every other visible element unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Tune a High-Precision Edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a mask-based inpaint with moderate strength and strong control. Match the nearby material pattern and keep all unmasked pixels visually stable.

output_examples.input: A street photo with a mask over overhead cables.
output_examples.output: The result removes the cables and fills the masked sky area with a consistent gradient.

output_examples.input: A product image with the background masked.
output_examples.output: The product remains unchanged while the masked background becomes a smooth studio surface.

output_examples.input: A photo with no mask and a prompt describing a small corner distraction.
output_examples.output: The selected distraction is removed using spatial language, with the rest of the image preserved.

best_practices: Use masks that match the source image and blur hard edges slightly.
State what must stay unchanged outside the mask.
Use lower strength for cleanup and higher strength for full replacement.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private or regulated images without approval for external processing.
Do not reuse a mask from a different image.
Do not ask for precise region edges without a mask when one can be provided.

faq.question: Does this skill run locally only?
faq.answer: No. It uses the local runcomfy CLI, but model processing happens through RunComfy endpoints.

faq.question: Do I need a mask image?
faq.answer: A mask is required for the default inpainting route. The skill can use description-based edit models when no mask exists.

faq.question: Can it create masks for me?
faq.answer: No. Create masks with another tool, then provide the mask URL to the CLI workflow.

faq.question: What data leaves my machine?
faq.answer: Image URLs, mask URLs, prompts, and job metadata are sent to RunComfy for model processing.

faq.question: Is the API token stored safely?
faq.answer: The skill documents local token storage with mode 0600. CI users can use the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable.

faq.question: Can it edit videos?
faq.answer: No. This skill covers still-image inpainting and points to a separate video inpainting workflow.
